 {"id":"bd-db300.6","title":"Track F: async pre-JIT and compiled-cache strategy","description":"## Goal
This track handles the user's pre-JIT hypothesis rigorously instead of hand-waving. The current JIT scaffold was pure overhead because it hashed programs and touched shared state on the hot path without ever producing a compiled fast path. A future compiled cache is only justified if statement repetition and compile amortization data show that interpreter-first plus asynchronous compilation can deliver net wins without contaminating latency.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The project has a clear go/no-go decision on background pre-JIT.
- Any implementation keeps compile work off the execute path.
- Correctness, cache invalidation, and latency effects are explicitly tested.
- This bead set explicitly includes comprehensive unit-test, end-to-end, and structured-logging obligations rather than treating verification as implied follow-up work.","design":"- Decide based on measured reuse patterns, not intuition.
- Keep the hot path interpreter-first and non-blocking.
- If a compiled cache exists, compilation must happen off-path with clear invalidation and size controls.","acceptance_criteria":"- The project has a clear go/no-go decision on background pre-JIT.
- Any implementation keeps compile work off the execute path.
- Correctness, cache invalidation, and latency effects are explicitly tested.
- This bead set explicitly includes comprehensive unit-test, end-to-end, and structured-logging obligations rather than treating verification as implied follow-up work.","notes":"This track is intentionally lower priority than the commit-path and synchronization tracks. It is a leverage bet, not the current root cause, and it must stay evidence-driven.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-10T04:47:12.443426654Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:51:16.166627157Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db300-program","db300-track-f","optimization","perf","prepared-statements","program","track-f","vdbe"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-db300.6.1","title":"F1: measure repeat-statement locality and compile amortization","description":"## Goal
Measure statement repetition, locality, and compile amortization so the project can decide whether an async pre-JIT path is actually worth building. This bead exists to keep the JIT idea evidence-driven instead of letting it become an attractive but unproven side quest, and it must account for whether reuse is lane-local or cross-core enough to justify background compilation without hurting front-end locality.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. The workload data shows how often statements repeat, what the reuse distance looks like, how much locality is lane-local versus cross-core or cross-node, and what compile cost would need to be amortized.
2. First-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, and any measurable background-compile interference on hot benchmark cells are captured.
3. The result is a clear go or no-go recommendation for background compilation, including whether any compiled cache should be per-lane, shared, or rejected.
4. The recommendation is grounded in the real benchmark fixtures and at least one nontrivial placement profile, not a toy microbenchmark alone.
5. The scripts, harness changes, or reports in this bead emit structured logs and artifact manifests rich enough to reproduce results without reconstructing the workflow by hand.","acceptance_criteria":"1. 3. The result is a clear go or no-go recommendation for background compilation, including whether any compiled cache should be per-lane, shared, or rejected.
4. The recommendation is grounded in the real benchmark fixtures and at least one nontrivial placement profile, not a toy microbenchmark alone.
5. The scripts, harness changes, or reports in this bead emit structured logs and artifact manifests rich enough to reproduce results without reconstructing the workflow by hand.","notes":"Because the current JIT scaffold was all overhead and no compiled fast path, this bead must set a high bar for adding complexity back in.  {"id":"bd-db300.6.2","title":"F2: design an interpreter-first async compile queue with zero execute-path blocking","description":"## Goal
Design the interpreter-first async compile queue and compiled-cache lifecycle so compile work can happen off the execute path without polluting latency-sensitive lanes, instruction cache, LLC locality, or TLB behavior. This bead should turn the hybrid idea into a concrete design with compile-debt budgeting, duplicate suppression, admission control, and helper-core placement instead of a vague background-JIT concept.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. The design states when a statement is eligible for background compilation and what locality or reuse evidence justifies spending cycles on it.
2. Compilation never blocks the current execution of the statement and runs under an explicit bounded queue or compile-debt budget tied to the many-core scheduling policy.
3. Cache fill, invalidation, duplicate-compile suppression, eviction, and concurrency behavior are explicit.
4. The design explains how compile work is placed to avoid harming foreground lanes, including whether it may share a core, LLC domain, or NUMA node with latency-sensitive execution.
5. The design defines metrics for first-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, compile success rate, queue depth, and background interference.
6. The resulting design or decision record includes an explicit verification plan describing the unit tests, end-to-end scenarios, and logging artifacts required for follow-on implementation.","acceptance_criteria":"1. The design states when a statement is eligible for background compilation and what locality or reuse evidence justifies spending cycles on it.

4. The design explains how compile work is placed to avoid harming foreground lanes, including whether it may share a core, LLC domain, or NUMA node with latency-sensitive execution.
5. The design defines metrics for first-hit latency, warmed-hit latency, compile success rate, queue depth, and background interference.
6. The resulting design or decision record includes an explicit verification plan describing the unit tests, end-to-end scenarios, and logging artifacts required for follow-on implementation.","notes":"This design should assume the interpreter remains the fallback and initial execution path. The compiled cache is an optimization layer, not a new mandatory dependency, and it must not quietly trade away responsiveness for possible future wins.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-10T05:07:38.715479949Z","created_by":"codex","updated_at":"2026-03-10T18:51:16.570381115Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","db300-program","db300-track-f","parallelism","prepared-statements","track-f","vdbe"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.5.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-db300.6.2","depends_on_id":"bd-db300.6.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-12T00:26:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

@@ -2008,7 +2008,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-u0v9e","title":"V2.1: Fused NewRowid+MakeRecord+Insert superinstruction for sequential append","description":"## What
Create a single superinstruction that combines the three hottest opcodes in the INSERT path: NewRowid (allocate rowid), MakeRecord (serialize record), Insert (write to B-tree). This bypasses the interpreter dispatch loop for the inner INSERT body.

## Why — The Alien Graveyard Connection
**Superinstruction fusion** is a well-known interpreter optimization from Forth (1970s), Tcl (2000s), and modern JavaScript engines. The idea: identify hot opcode sequences at compile time and replace them with a single fused operation. This eliminates N-1 dispatch cycles (match, pc increment, bounds check) for an N-opcode sequence.

For a typical prepared INSERT, the inner body is:
```
Variable ?1 → r[1]     // bind param
NewRowid → r[2]         // allocate rowid  
MakeRecord r[1] → r[3]  // serialize
Insert cursor0 r[3] r[2] // write B-tree
```
This is 4 opcodes × ~800ns avg dispatch = ~3.2µs. A fused superinstruction does the same work in a single dispatch: ~800ns for the B-tree write + ~200ns for serialization = ~1µs. **Saves ~2.2µs per row (~55% of per-row overhead).**

## How
1. **Detection at compile time**: In `fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs`, after generating the opcode stream for INSERT, scan for the pattern `[NewRowid(p1=C, p2=R_rowid), MakeRecord(p1=R_start, p2=N, p3=R_record), Insert(p1=C, p2=R_record, p3=R_rowid)]` where C is the same cursor and R_record/R_rowid match. Replace with a single `FusedAppendInsert(cursor, R_start, N, flags)` opcode.

2. **Opcode handler**: Add `Opcode::FusedAppendInsert` variant. The handler:
   - Allocates rowid: `sc.last_alloc_rowid + 1` (using the NewRowid cache optimization already landed)
   - Serializes directly from registers into `make_record_buf` (existing hot path)
   - Inserts via `table_insert_prechecked_absent` (append mode, no seek needed)
   - Updates changes counter, last_insert_rowid, all bookkeeping
   All in a single function call, zero dispatch overhead.

3. **Fallback**: If the cursor is not in append mode or conditions change (conflict, page split requiring seek), fall back to the individual opcodes.

## Alien Graveyard References
- Superinstruction fusion: Tcl bytecode compiler (1999), Dalvik VM (2008)
- Instruction combining: GCC's combine pass, LLVM's InstCombine
- This is also related to "macro-op fusion" in CPU microarchitecture (Intel Sandybridge fusing CMP+JCC)

## Files
- `crates/fsqlite-types/src/opcode.rs` — add FusedAppendInsert variant to Opcode enum
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs` — pattern detection + replacement pass
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs` — handler for FusedAppendInsert in hot path

## Estimated impact
~2.2µs saved per INSERT row = 22ms at 10K rows = **55% of per-row overhead**
This is the single highest-EV optimization remaining.

## Verification
- Codegen unit tests: verify pattern detection
- EXPLAIN output: verify FusedAppendInsert appears for simple INSERTs
- Conformance oracle suite 
- Benchmark section 1 (INSERT) — target: 40ms → ~18ms at 10K rows

 {"id":"bd-u43hx","title":"Flat Combining for lock contention hot spots (§14.2)","description":"Implement Flat Combining (Hendler et al. 2010) for lock contention hot spots. Flat Combining turns contention into throughput: instead of N threads each acquiring a lock, one combiner thread collects all N requests and executes them in a batch, eliminating lock handoff overhead.

## Mathematical Foundation
Under high contention (N threads, lock hold time H), traditional locking throughput is approximately 1/H (one operation per lock hold). Flat Combining throughput is approximately N/H (batch of N operations per lock hold). The improvement is proportional to contention level.

## Application in FrankenSQLite
- WAL append (before parallel WAL is implemented)
- Buffer pool eviction queue
- SSI conflict table updates
- Any centralized data structure under high contention

## Alien CS Reference
Hendler, Incze, Shavit, Tzafrir 2010 Flat Combining and the Synchronization-Parallelism Tradeoff: original paper with formal throughput analysis and experimental results showing 2-10x improvement under high contention. Fatourou and Kallimanis 2012 Revisiting the Combining Synchronization Technique: optimized combining with reduced publication list overhead.

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Define FlatCombiner<Op, Result> as a generic framework
- Publication list: Vec<AtomicPtr<CombinerSlot<Op, Result>>> with per-thread slots via thread_local!
- Combiner election: try_lock on a central Mutex; winner becomes combiner, losers park
- Batch execution: combiner iterates publication list, collects pending Ops, executes batch, writes Results
- Thread parking: use std::thread::park/unpark for efficient waiting (avoid spin loops)
- Slot lifecycle: allocate on first use, reuse across operations (avoid repeated allocation)
- Use Pin<Box<CombinerSlot>> to ensure stable addresses for AtomicPtr

## Logging Requirements
- Log combiner election at tracing::debug level: thread_id, batch_size
- Log batch execution at tracing::debug level: ops_count, elapsed_time
- Log contention metrics at tracing::info level periodically: avg_batch_size, combiner_switches_per_sec
- Log fallback to direct execution (if batch size = 1) at tracing::trace level

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Flat Combining implementation (generic combiner framework)
- [ ] Publication list with per-thread slots
- [ ] Combiner election and batch execution
- [ ] Benchmark: Flat Combining vs mutex under 32-thread contention
- [ ] Miri clean","acceptance_criteria":"1. Implementation is complete and behaviorally correct against explicit invariants and edge-case requirements, including fault and recovery paths where applicable. 2. Comprehensive unit/property tests cover normal, boundary, and adversarial conditions with deterministic fixtures and regression seeds. 3. End-to-end scenario scripts validate integrated behavior in real execution flows and include deterministic replay instructions. 4. Structured logging/metrics (traceable IDs, timing, counters, error context) are emitted and validated so failures are diagnosable and auditable.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"MagentaDesert (agent-8)","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:18.756719865Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-21T06:42:04.649124137Z","closed_at":"2026-02-21T06:42:04.649100863Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","alien-pass-2026-02-13","concurrency","ev-gated"],"comments":[{"id":2035,"issue_id":"bd-u43hx","author":"MagentaDesert (agent-8)","text":"## Evidence: bd-u43hx Flat Combining for lock contention hot spots (§14.2)

### Implementation
- `crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/flat_combining.rs` (656 lines)
  - `FlatCombiner` with `MAX_FC_THREADS=64` slots, AtomicU64-based publication list
  - `FcHandle` per-thread handle with `apply`, `add`, `read` operations
  - Combiner election via `try_lock`, batch execution, result publication
  - Global metrics: batches_total, ops_total, batch_size_sum/max, wait_ns_total/max
  - 10 unit tests (register/unregister, single-thread, concurrent, stress, metrics, batching, read, starvation, debug)

### Harness integration tests
- `crates/fsqlite-harness/tests/bd_u43hx_flat_combining_contention.rs` (10 tests)
  1. Contention-to-throughput: 16 threads * 500 adds = 8000, exact total
  2. FC vs Mutex latency comparison under 4-thread contention
  3. High thread registration: 64/64 slots, overflow rejection
  4. Read-dominated workload: 282:1 read:write ratio, monotonic reads
  5. Mixed add/read contention: 4 writers + 4 readers, exact total
  6. Metrics fidelity: delta-based ops/batches tracking
  7. Slot reuse: drop-and-re-register, value persistence
  8. Throughput scaling: 1/2/4/8 threads characterization
  9. Accumulator monotonicity: 86K checks, never decreases
  10. Conformance summary: 6/6 properties verified (100%)

### CI verification
- `scripts/verify_bd_u43hx_flat_combining_contention.sh`

### Test results
```
running 10 tests
test test_contention_to_throughput ... ok
test test_fc_vs_mutex_latency ... ok
test test_high_thread_registration ... ok
test test_read_dominated_workload ... ok
test test_mixed_add_read_contention ... ok
test test_metrics_fidelity ... ok
test test_slot_reuse ... ok
test test_throughput_scaling ... ok
```

Provenance: the original spec extract and rationale remain in this bead's comments.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:55:32.919879274Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T17:57:42.231361967Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T06:25:11.817366094Z","close_reason":"Content merged into bd-1zla (P1 §6.8-6.10)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-u49k","depends_on_id":"bd-16ks","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-u49k","depends_on_id":"bd-7pu","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2036,"issue_id":"bd-u49k","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §6.9 Memory Accounting (System-Wide, No Surprise OOM)

Every subsystem storing variable-size state MUST have: strict byte budget, reclamation policy under pressure, metrics exported for harness + benchmarks. No unbounded growth accepted.

**System-wide memory budget table:**
| Subsystem | Budget Source | Reclamation Policy |
|---|---|---|
| ARC page cache | PRAGMA cache_size | ARC eviction (S6.3-6.4) |
| Transaction write sets (page images) | PRAGMA fsqlite.txn_write_set_mem_bytes | Spill to per-txn temp file (S5.9.2); abort if spill I/O fails |
| MVCC page version chains | GC horizon (min active snapshot) | Coalescing + version drop (S6.7) |
| SSI witness plane (hot+cold) | Hot: fixed SHM layout; Cold: fixed byte budgets | Hot: epoch swap (S5.6.4.8); Cold: LRU + rebuild from ECS; evidence GC by safe horizons |
| Symbol caches (decoded objects) | Fixed byte budget, configurable | LRU eviction |
| Index segment caches | Fixed byte budget | LRU eviction; rebuild from ECS on miss |
| Bloom/quotient filters | O(n) where n = active pages with versions | Rebuilt on GC horizon advance |

**Cache tracks total_bytes not just page count** because MVCC version chain compression (sparse XOR deltas, S3.4.4) produces variable-size entries. Full page = 4096B; sparse delta may be ~200B.

**Dual eviction trigger:** Fires when EITHER page count > capacity OR total_bytes > max_bytes. Prevents memory exhaustion when many full-size pages cached alongside compact deltas.

## §6.10 PRAGMA cache_size Mapping

N > 0: capacity = N, max_bytes = N * page_size.
N < 0: max_bytes = |N| * 1024 (KiB), capacity = max_bytes / page_size.
N == 0: capacity = 0, max_bytes = 0. NO special "reset to default" logic — compile-time default (SQLITE_DEFAULT_CACHE_SIZE = -2000) only applied at database open time.

Default: -2000 (= 2000 KiB). For 4096B pages -> 500 pages (2 MiB). For 1024B pages -> 2000 pages. Ghost lists limited to capacity entries each (~72KB overhead for 2000 entries).

**Resize protocol (runtime change):** (1) Set new capacity and max_bytes, (2) If |T1|+|T2| > new_capacity: repeatedly call REPLACE until within limits, (3) Trim ghost lists: B1.truncate(new_capacity), B2.truncate(new_capacity), (4) Clamp p to [0, new_capacity].

## §6.11 Performance Analysis

| Workload | Pages | Hot | Cache | H(LRU) | H(ARC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLTP point queries | 100K | 500 | 2000 | 0.96 | 0.97 |
| Mixed OLTP + scan | 100K | 500 | 2000 | 0.60 | 0.85 |
| Full table scan | 100K | 100K | 2000 | 0.02 | 0.02 |
| Zipfian (s=1.0) | 100K | N/A | 2000 | 0.82 | 0.89 |
| MVCC 8 writers | 100K | 800 | 2000 | 0.55 | 0.78 |

ARC advantage most pronounced in mixed workloads. T2 protects frequently-accessed pages from scan pollution. Under MVCC with multiple writers, ARC naturally separates hot current versions (T2) from cold superseded versions (evicted or coalesced).

## §6.12 Warm-Up Behavior

Phase 1 — Cold start (0 to ~50% full): All misses. p=0. No adaptation.
Phase 2 — Learning (~50-100% full): First evictions. Ghost lists populate. p adapts toward workload. Hit rate climbs 20-60%.
Phase 3 — Steady state (full): p converged. Hit rate at expected value. Reached after approximately 3x capacity accesses.

**Pre-warming (optional, PRAGMA cache_warm = ON):** On database open, read pages referenced in WAL index into T1 (limited to half capacity). Also read root pages of all tables/indexes from sqlite_master.

-{"id":"bd-u6u1q","title":"Track R: Zero-Alloc Record Encoding — Match C SQLite OP_MakeRecord In-Place Write Pattern","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:30.580187495Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:34.456490967Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-u6u1q","depends_on_id":"bd-ec8km","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:34.064824966Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-u6u1q","depends_on_id":"bd-gieaf","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:34.455715672Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-u6u1q","depends_on_id":"bd-pt17g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:33.665665396Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3435,"issue_id":"bd-u6u1q","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Problem (from C SQLite analysis: vdbe.c OP_MakeRecord)
C SQLite's MakeRecord writes directly into a pre-allocated register buffer. Zero heap allocations. 2 passes over data (header + body). Total cost: ~20 CPU cycles per row.

FrankenSQLite's serialize_record_iter (record.rs:844) allocates a new Vec<u8> per record, iterates values 3 times (serial types, header, body), and calls 3-4 functions per column value. Total cost: ~120 CPU cycles per row.

## Solution: Write records directly into a reusable engine-owned buffer
1. VdbeEngine owns a `record_buf: Vec<u8>` that persists across rows
2. MakeRecord opcode writes directly into this buffer (clear + refill, no realloc)
3. Reduce to 2 passes: compute header in-place, then write body
4. Inline serial_type_for_value/encode_value as a single match arm per type

## C SQLite Reference: vdbe.c OP_MakeRecord
- Writes into `pOut->z` which is Mem.zMalloc (pre-allocated, reused across rows)
- Single function: sqlite3VdbeSerialPut() writes value at offset
- Header and body written in same pass for fixed-width types

## Expected Impact: -100 cycles per row (from 120 → 20 cycles)
For 10K rows: saves ~1 ms = ~25% of current INSERT time

## Where
- crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs (MakeRecord opcode handler)
- crates/fsqlite-types/src/record.rs (serialize_record_iter → serialize_record_into_buf)
","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:31:30Z"}]}
+{"id":"bd-u6u1q","title":"Track R: Zero-Alloc Record Encoding — Match C SQLite OP_MakeRecord In-Place Write Pattern","notes":"Epic complete: All sub-tasks closed. R1 (reusable record buffer) committed 707bb028, R2 (two-pass encoding) deferred as already optimal, TR (tests) verified scratch reuse and 128-column roundtrip.  {"id":"bd-udl9m","title":"Track G: Zero-Seek Sequential Insert — Eliminate 0.25-0.35 µs B-tree Seek Overhead","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:25:36.092124594Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T18:49:43.934289874Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","depends_on_id":"bd-0zxi6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:51:12.388895999Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","depends_on_id":"bd-2mpt9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:51:13.544643501Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","depends_on_id":"bd-s9k7k","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:51:12.968149404Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3366,"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Problem
Every INSERT does root-to-leaf B-tree traversal even for monotonically increasing rowids (the most common case). C SQLite has BTREE_APPEND optimization that skips the seek entirely when the new rowid > max existing rowid. FrankenSQLite has this only in serialized mode, NOT in concurrent mode.

## Solution: Rightmost Leaf Pointer + Append-Only Fast Path
Maintain a `rightmost_leaf: Option<(PageNumber, usize)>` in StorageCursor. For sequential inserts (detected by rowid > last_rowid), go directly to the rightmost leaf and append. Zero seek, zero binary search, zero page traversal.

## Alien Graveyard Entries Referenced:
- §5.3 B-link Trees (Lehman-Yao) — rightmost pointer chaining
- §4.7 Log-Structured Merge Trees — append-only insert batching concept

## Expected Impact: -0.25 to -0.35 µs per sequential INSERT (eliminates B-tree descent entirely)
For random-key inserts: no change (falls back to normal seek).

## Key Insight
95%+ of real-world INSERTs use auto-increment rowids → monotonically increasing → ALWAYS sequential. This optimization applies to the vast majority of workloads.
","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:25:36Z"},{"id":3519,"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","author":"ubuntu","text":"# bd-udl9m progress

- Read `AGENTS.md` and confirmed the hard rules: no file deletion, keep `concurrent_mode_default` true, no `unsafe`, no Tokio, manual edits only.
- Reviewed `br show bd-udl9m` and traced the current append path through `fsqlite-vdbe` and `fsqlite-btree`.
- Identified a concrete gap: prepared/direct paths and retained cursor machinery exist, but the generic `execute_table_program_with_cx` DML path still disables retained storage-cursor reuse, so plain repeated `INSERT` statements rebuild the storage cursor and lose the right-edge append hint across statements.
- Implementing a narrow fix on the generic DML path plus a regression test that exercises repeated non-prepared sequential inserts across statements.
","created_at":"2026-04-10T17:50:01Z"},{"id":3524,"issue_id":"bd-udl9m","author":"ubuntu","text":"## bd-udl9m progress

Summary:
- Read `/data/projects/frankensqlite/AGENTS.md` and kept the hard constraints in force: `concurrent_mode_default` stays `true`, no `unsafe`, no Tokio ecosystem, manual edits only, no file deletion.
- Re-read `br show bd-udl9m` and traced the sequential insert path through `fsqlite-core`, `fsqlite-vdbe`, and the prepared/direct-insert dispatch.
- Reapplied the missing generic DML fix in `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs`: `execute_table_program_with_cx` now lets DML executions retain storage cursors by threading `allow_retained_cursor_reuse = invalidate_memdb_count_shortcuts_on_success` into `execute_table_program_with_db(...)` instead of hard-coding `false`.
- Added a regression test in `crates/fsqlite-core/tests/v2_superinstruction_tests.rs` that targets repeated sequential ad-hoc `INSERT` statements on the reusable table-program lane rather than the direct compiled insert lane.

Verification:
- `cargo check --workspace --all-targets`

- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`
  - Passed on 2026-04-10.

- `timeout 180s cargo test -p fsqlite-core --test v2_superinstruction_tests test_v2_plain_execute_sequential_inserts_keep_append_path_hot_across_statements -- --nocapture`
  - Blocked before reaching the bd-udl9m test body by a pre-existing unrelated `fsqlite-pager` test-build failure:
    - `crates/fsqlite-pager/src/pager.rs`: unused import `TransactionFrameBatchContext`

Constraints held:
- `concurrent_mode_default` remains `true`
- no `unsafe_code`

- manual edits only

 {"id":"bd-ujkm","title":"5D.1: Wire COMMIT to use pager transaction commit (replace compat_persist)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:49:52.707566933Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T05:36:22.770013263Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T05:36:22.769995099Z","close_reason":"COMMIT no longer calls compat_persist; pager commit is now the persistence path with passing regression checks","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ujkm","depends_on_id":"bd-1yi8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2038,"issue_id":"bd-ujkm","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# 5D.1: Wire COMMIT to use pager transaction commit (replace compat_persist)

## What

Currently, COMMIT calls `txn.commit()` on the pager transaction BUT THEN also calls
`persist_if_needed()` which runs `compat_persist` — a function that serializes the entire
MemDatabase to a SQLite file from scratch. The pager commit is essentially a no-op because
the real persistence comes from compat_persist.

This task makes pager transaction commit the REAL persistence path.

## Where to change

**Primary:** `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` — `execute_commit()`

## Current code

```rust
fn execute_commit(&self) -> Result<()> {
    let cx = Cx::background();

    if let Some(mut txn) = self.active_txn.borrow_mut().take() {
        txn.commit(&cx)?;  // Pager commit (WAL append) — but this is empty/unused
    }

    self.db.borrow_mut().commit_undo();  // MemDatabase undo log committed
    *self.in_transaction.borrow_mut() = false;
    self.savepoints.borrow_mut().clear();

    self.persist_if_needed()?;  // compat_persist: REWRITE ENTIRE FILE FROM MEMDB
    Ok(())
}
```

## Target code

```rust
fn execute_commit(&self) -> Result<()> {
    let cx = Cx::background();

    if let Some(mut txn) = self.active_txn.borrow_mut().take() {
        // THIS is now the real persistence event
        // All dirty pages written to WAL by the pager
        txn.commit(&cx)?;
    }

    // MemDatabase shadow: mark undo committed (still useful during transition)
    self.db.borrow_mut().commit_undo();
    *self.in_transaction.borrow_mut() = false;
    self.savepoints.borrow_mut().clear();

    // NO persist_if_needed() — pager commit IS the persistence
    // Data is now durable in WAL (fsync'd per PRAGMA synchronous setting)

    Ok(())
}
```

## What pager commit actually does

When TransactionHandle::commit() is called:
1. All dirty pages in the transaction's write-set are appended as WAL frames
2. The last frame gets a nonzero "database size" field (commit marker)
3. Cumulative checksums ensure frame integrity
4. fsync() the WAL file (if PRAGMA synchronous = FULL)
5. Update the WAL index (shared memory) so readers can see the new frames
6. Release the writer lock

After commit, the data is durable. Crash recovery replays WAL frames to reconstruct state.

## Tests

- `test_commit_persists_through_pager`: INSERT+COMMIT, close, reopen, data present
- `test_commit_without_compat_persist`: Verify compat_persist is NOT called
- `test_commit_survives_crash_simulation`: Write WAL frames, truncate, recover
- `test_multiple_commits`: Three transactions, each commit appends to WAL
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:51:51Z"},{"id":2039,"issue_id":"bd-ujkm","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented 5D.1 in crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs by removing compat persistence call from Statement::Commit arm. COMMIT now routes exclusively through execute_commit() (pager TransactionHandle::commit) and no longer invokes persist_if_needed/compat_persist afterward. Validation run: cargo test -p fsqlite-core test_persist_ -- --nocapture; cargo test -p fsqlite-core begin_commit -- --nocapture; cargo fmt --check; cargo check --all-targets; cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings.","created_at":"2026-02-12T05:36:22Z"}]}
